item.description:
Body Image

Oprah, Ozempic, and Our Obsession With Weight

Public scrutiny of Oprah’s body reflects the persistence of weight stigma.

Posted March 10, 2026

Few public figures have had their bodies scrutinized as intensely as Oprah Winfrey. For decades, her weight has been treated as a public narrative, discussed, analyzed, and debated almost as much as her groundbreaking career. Oprah’s story is not just about one celebrity’s body. It reflects something much bigger: the cultural obsession with weight, the pressures placed on women in the public eye, and the powerful ways weight stigma shapes how we talk about health.

The Weight of Public Scrutiny

From the early years of The Oprah Winfrey Show, Oprah spoke openly about her struggles with dieting and weight fluctuations. One of the most memorable moments came in 1988, when she wheeled a wagon filled with animal fat onto the stage to represent the weight she had lost on a liquid diet. At the time, it was framed as a triumphant transformation. Today, it illustrates how deeply diet culture shaped the public conversation about bodies. Oprah's weight loss was presented not just as a personal achievement, but as a moral victory. And because she was one of the most visible women in media, her body became a cultural battleground for ideas about weight and health. When someone lives in the spotlight, weight changes become headlines and fodder for commentary amid endless “before-and-after” photos. Many people experience this dynamic on a smaller scale.

Body Comments and Mental Health

Weight-related comments, whether framed as concern, praise, or casual observation, can carry powerful psychological effects. These effects include low self-esteem, depressive symptoms, and disordered eating behaviors, which are most prominent for individuals with existing body dissatisfaction. When we normalize commenting on people's weight we reinforce the idea that body size is something to constantly monitor, evaluate, and discuss. In other words, the same cultural forces that turned Oprah’s body into a public storyline are the ones shaping everyday conversations in families, schools, and workplaces. And those messages can accumulate over time, shaping how people see themselves and their worth.

When Health Gets Reduced to Weight

Another part of Oprah’s public story reflects a broader cultural misconception: the idea that health should be automatically equated with body size. For decades, popular media promoted the idea that weight loss was the primary pathway to "getting healthy." Diets, programs, and lifestyle regimens promised not only better physical health but greater happiness, more confidence, and better relationships. More recently, the conversation has begun to shift. Oprah herself has spoken about the emotional toll of years spent trying to control her weight. Her reflections echo what many people are beginning to recognize: health is far more complex than a number on the scale. Weight-centric thinking can obscure the many other factors that influence health, including genetics, stress, socioeconomic status, access to healthcare, and social stigma.

"Enough": GLP-1 Medications and a New Phase of the Conversation

More recently, Oprah has entered the conversation surrounding a new generation of weight-loss medications known as GLP-1 receptor agonists, including drugs such as Ozempic and Wegovy. After decades of public discussion about diets, willpower, and lifestyle change, she shared publicly that she had begun using a weight-loss medication, an admission that sparked widespread media attention. In part, this was because it disrupted the long-standing cultural narrative that weight is simply a matter of personal responsibility and discipline.

The rise of GLP-1 medications has complicated the story we tell about weight. These treatments highlight the powerful biological systems that regulate appetite, metabolism, and fat storage—systems that cannot simply be overridden through willpower. Oprah reflects on this shift in Enough: Your Weight, Your Health, and What It’s Like to Be Free, written with physician-scientist Ania M. Jastreboff. In the book, she describes the emotional burden of trying to control her weight under constant public scrutiny and the freedom that came from realizing that her weight was never something she could fully control in the first place.

At the same time, the intense fascination with celebrity use of these medications reveals that society’s fixation on weight has not disappeared; it has simply evolved. The conversation has shifted from “Which diet works?” to “Which medication works?” Yet the underlying message often remains the same: Smaller bodies are inherently preferable. The book’s central theme pushes beyond this narrative. The title itself—Enough—raises a deeper question about worth and freedom. For many people, the most meaningful shift may not come from finally “fixing” their bodies, but from rejecting the belief that their value or character depends on body size at all.

A Cultural Mirror

Oprah’s relationship with weight has often been framed as a personal struggle. But it may be more accurate to see it as a cultural mirror. Her story reveals how deeply weight stigma is embedded in our society. It shows how even the most powerful and successful women are not exempt from body scrutiny. And it reminds us how easily conversations about health become conversations about appearance.

The real question may not be why Oprah’s weight has fluctuated over the years, but why we have been so invested in the size of her body.

Moving the Conversation Forward

If Oprah’s public journey tells us anything, it is that decades of dieting, body commentary, and surveillance have not produced the kind of health or peace that diet culture promises. A different conversation might begin by shifting attention away from body size and toward well-being, dignity, and respect. It might involve recognizing that comments about weight, even if well-intentioned, can have unintended consequences. And it might require challenging the deeply ingrained assumption that bodies must always be judged and improved.

For decades, Oprah has shaped cultural conversations about everything from literature to mental health. Perhaps her evolving openness about weight, biology, and self-acceptance can also help shift how we think about bodies themselves. Because the issue was never really Oprah’s weight.

It was our culture’s obsession with it.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Sophie S. Whynacht Ph.D.
